pages.title: Changelog
pages.description: Release history for @claritylabs/cl-sdk
pages.content:
All notable changes to `@claritylabs/cl-sdk` are documented here.

## Unreleased — Extraction Completeness

### Added

- **First-class definitions extraction** — new `definitions` extractor and `document.definitions[]` field for defined terms, full definition text, page/form provenance, section references, and source snippets.
- **First-class covered reasons extraction** — new `covered_reasons` extractor and `document.coveredReasons[]` field for covered causes of loss, covered losses, inclusions, qualifying reasons, attached conditions/exceptions, and provenance.
- **Dedicated retrieval chunks** — new `definition` and `covered_reason` chunk types. Covered-reason conditions are emitted as separate `covered_reason` chunks for precise retrieval.
- **Quality checks** — deterministic warnings for mapped definitions/covered-reason pages with empty structured output, missing definition/covered-reason content, missing page provenance, and referential covered-reason text.

### Changed

- **Extraction pipeline now has 13 focused extractors** with page-map routing and review follow-up support for `definitions` and `covered_reasons`.
- **Formatter coverage expanded** to definition text plus covered-reason content and conditions.
- **Financial promotion expanded** to more premium and total-cost aliases and declaration-derived `taxesAndFees[]`, including common GST/HST/PST/QST-style fields.

## v0.11.0 — Declarations Promotion & Summary Generation

### Added

- **Declarations-to-schema promotion pass** — new Phase 7 in the extraction pipeline. After assembly, a promotion pass fills top-level typed fields from extracted declarations and coverage arrays. This bridges the gap between detailed extraction (100+ declaration fields, dozens of coverages) and the typed `InsuranceDocument` schema. Runs seven sub-passes with no model calls:
  - **Carrier field mapping** — `naicNumber` → `carrierNaicNumber`, `admittedStatus` → `carrierAdmittedStatus`, `amBestRating` → `carrierAmBestRating`; builds `insurer` sub-object
  - **Broker promotion** — declarations broker/agent/producer → `brokerAgency`, `brokerContactName`, `producer`
  - **Loss payee / mortgage holder promotion** — declarations → `lossPayees[]`, `mortgageHolders[]`
  - **Location promotion** — groups declaration location fields → `locations[]`
  - **Limits synthesis** — coverage names → `limits` schedule (GL, auto, umbrella, WC patterns)
  - **Deductible synthesis** — most common deductible → `deductibles.perOccurrence`; detects SIR and aggregate
  - **Premium fallback** — declarations premium → `premium` when extractor missed it
  - New file: `src/extraction/promote.ts`
- **Summary generation** — new Phase 8. Generates a 1–3 sentence AI summary of the assembled document. Appears as `summary` on the final `InsuranceDocument`. Gracefully skipped on failure.
  - New file: `src/prompts/coordinator/summarize.ts`
- **Broker extraction in `carrier_info`** — extractor now captures `brokerAgency`, `brokerContactName`, and `brokerLicenseNumber` alongside carrier data
- **Loss payee / mortgage holder extraction in `named_insured`** — extractor now captures `lossPayees[]` and `mortgageHolders[]` alongside insured data

### Fixed

- **Field name inconsistency** — carrier extractor output used short names (`naicNumber`, `admittedStatus`) that didn't match the `PolicyDocument` schema's prefixed names (`carrierNaicNumber`, `carrierAdmittedStatus`). The promotion pass now maps these correctly.

### Changed

- Pipeline now runs **10 phases** (was 8): Classify → Page Map → Plan → Extract → Review → Assemble → **Promote** → **Summarize** → Format → Chunk

## v0.7.x — Schema Compatibility & Resilience

### Added

- **`toStrictSchema()`** — auto-transforms Zod schemas before `generateObject` calls, converting `.optional()` to `.nullable()` for OpenAI strict structured output compatibility. Applied automatically in the pipeline.
- **`safeGenerateObject()`** — wraps `generateObject` with schema strictification, retry on schema validation errors, and optional fallback values when all retries are exhausted.
- **Pipeline checkpoints** — `createPipelineContext()` for save/resume at phase boundaries with `onCheckpointSave` callback.

### Fixed

- **`z.record()` removal** — replaced all `z.record()` usages in schemas with `z.array(z.object({ key, value }))` to avoid unsupported `propertyNames` in JSON Schema.
- **Transient error retry** — `withRetry()` now retries on grammar compilation timeouts, no-output-generated, overloaded, and 5xx errors (not just rate limits).
- **Graceful degradation** — extraction pipeline uses fallback values for classify/plan/review when all retries fail, allowing the pipeline to continue rather than crash.

## v0.5.0

### Added

- **Markdown formatting pass** — new Phase 6 in the extraction pipeline. After assembly, a formatting agent cleans up all content-bearing fields (sections, endorsements, exclusions, conditions, summary) to ensure proper markdown rendering. Fixes pipe tables missing separator rows, space-aligned tables, sub-items mixed into tables, and general whitespace issues.
  - New files: `src/extraction/formatter.ts`, `src/prompts/coordinator/format.ts`
  - Pipeline now runs 7 phases: Classify → Plan → Extract → Review → Assemble → Format → Chunk
  - Token usage from formatting is tracked and included in the total

## v0.2.0 — Upcoming

### Changes

- **BREAKING:** `models` parameter is now required for all extraction functions (`ExtractOptions`, `ClassifyOptions`, `ExtractSectionsOptions`)
- **BREAKING:** Removed `createDefaultModelConfig()` — consumers must provide their own models
- **BREAKING:** Removed `SONNET_MODEL` and `HAIKU_MODEL` constants
- Added `pdfContentFormat` and `convertPdfToImages` options for PDF input format control
- Added `tokenLimits` option for per-role token limit overrides
- `MODEL_TOKEN_LIMITS` is now deprecated — use `DEFAULT_TOKEN_LIMITS`
- Fixed default metadata token limit to 16,384 (was incorrectly documented as 4,096)

## v0.1.0 — 2026-04-11

Version reset. All prior versions (1.x through 6.x) are no longer relevant and should not be used. The SDK will remain below 1.0.0 until official launch.

### Current Features

- Document classification (`classifyDocumentType`)
- PDF extraction pipeline (`extractFromPdf`, `extractQuoteFromPdf`)
- Provider-agnostic model configuration via Vercel AI SDK `LanguageModel` instances
- Composable agent prompt system (`buildAgentSystemPrompt`)
- Platform and intent model for multi-channel agents
- PDF operations (AcroForm fill, text overlay, page splitting)
- Comprehensive insurance type system (42 policy types, declarations, coverages)
- Rate-limit retry with exponential backoff
- Parallel chunk extraction with configurable concurrency
- Token usage tracking via `onTokenUsage` callback
- Context key mapping for policy-to-application auto-fill
- Native PDF file support with `convertPdfToImages` fallback

pages.title: Extraction Results
pages.description: Working with InsuranceDocument, DocumentChunk, and token usage output from the extraction pipeline
pages.content:
The extraction pipeline returns a fully typed `ExtractionResult` containing the structured document, retrieval-ready chunks, cumulative token usage, and usage-reporting metadata. The pipeline's promotion pass automatically fills top-level typed fields from declarations data and coverage arrays, so no post-processing or normalization step is needed — results are ready for persistence and querying.

## ExtractionResult

```typescript
interface ExtractionResult {
  document: InsuranceDocument;  // Fully typed policy or quote
  chunks: DocumentChunk[];       // Ready for vector storage
  tokenUsage: TokenUsage;        // Cumulative { inputTokens, outputTokens }
  usageReporting: {
    modelCalls: number;
    callsWithUsage: number;
    callsMissingUsage: number;
  };
}
```

```typescript
const { document, chunks, tokenUsage, usageReporting } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64, "doc-123");
```

## InsuranceDocument

`InsuranceDocument` is a discriminated union of `PolicyDocument` and `QuoteDocument`, distinguished by the `type` field:

```typescript
type InsuranceDocument = PolicyDocument | QuoteDocument;
```

Both types share a large set of base fields and diverge on type-specific fields.

### Shared fields

These fields appear on both `PolicyDocument` and `QuoteDocument`:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `id` | `string` | Document ID |
| `type` | `"policy" \| "quote"` | Discriminator |
| `carrier` | `string` | Insurance carrier name |
| `carrierLegalName` | `string?` | Legal entity name |
| `carrierNaicNumber` | `string?` | NAIC company code |
| `carrierAmBestRating` | `string?` | AM Best financial strength rating |
| `carrierAdmittedStatus` | `string?` | Admitted / non-admitted / surplus lines |
| `security` | `string?` | Security / surplus lines carrier |
| `mga` | `string?` | Managing General Agent |
| `underwriter` | `string?` | Underwriter name |
| `brokerAgency` | `string?` | Broker agency name |
| `brokerContactName` | `string?` | Individual producer name |
| `brokerLicenseNumber` | `string?` | Producer license number |
| `insuredName` | `string` | Name of the insured |
| `insuredDba` | `string?` | DBA name |
| `insuredAddress` | `Address?` | Mailing address |
| `insuredEntityType` | `EntityType?` | Legal entity type |
| `insuredFein` | `string?` | Federal EIN |
| `insuredSicCode` | `string?` | SIC code |
| `insuredNaicsCode` | `string?` | NAICS code |
| `additionalNamedInsureds` | `NamedInsured[]?` | Additional named insureds |
| `policyTypes` | `string[]?` | Lines of business |
| `premium` | `string?` | Total premium |
| `totalCost` | `string?` | Premium + taxes + fees |
| `summary` | `string?` | AI-generated 1–3 sentence overview (populated by the Summarize phase) |
| `coverages` | `Coverage[]` | Coverage details with limits |
| `enrichedCoverages` | `EnrichedCoverage[]?` | Detailed coverage data |
| `definitions` | `Definition[]?` | Defined terms with full definition text and page/form provenance |
| `coveredReasons` | `CoveredReason[]?` | Covered causes, covered losses, inclusions, qualifying reasons, attached conditions/exceptions, and provenance |
| `endorsements` | `Endorsement[]?` | Structured endorsements |
| `exclusions` | `Exclusion[]?` | Structured exclusions |
| `conditions` | `PolicyCondition[]?` | Structured conditions |
| `declarations` | `Declarations?` | Line-specific structured declarations |
| `sections` | `Section[]?` | Raw document sections |
| `limits` | `LimitSchedule?` | Structured limits schedule (synthesized from coverages by Promote phase) |
| `deductibles` | `DeductibleSchedule?` | Structured deductible schedule (synthesized from coverages by Promote phase) |
| `locations` | `InsuredLocation[]?` | Insured premises (promoted from declarations by Promote phase) |
| `vehicles` | `InsuredVehicle[]?` | Insured vehicles |
| `classifications` | `ClassificationCode[]?` | Rating class codes |
| `formInventory` | `FormReference[]?` | All form numbers |
| `taxesAndFees` | `TaxFeeItem[]?` | Taxes and fees breakdown |
| `coverageForm` | `CoverageForm?` | Occurrence, claims-made, or accident |
| `retroactiveDate` | `string?` | Retroactive date (claims-made) |
| `insurer` | `InsurerInfo?` | Structured insurer details |
| `producer` | `ProducerInfo?` | Structured producer details |
| `claimsContacts` | `Contact[]?` | Claims contact information |
| `regulatoryContacts` | `Contact[]?` | Regulatory contacts |
| `thirdPartyAdministrators` | `Contact[]?` | TPA contacts |
| `supplementaryFacts` | `{ key: string; value: string; subject?: string; context?: string }[]?` | Hidden retrieval-only facts captured by the full-document supplementary pass |
| `additionalInsureds` | `EndorsementParty[]?` | Additional insureds |
| `lossPayees` | `EndorsementParty[]?` | Loss payees (promoted from declarations and named insured extractor) |
| `mortgageHolders` | `EndorsementParty[]?` | Mortgage holders (promoted from declarations and named insured extractor) |
| `paymentPlan` | `PaymentPlan?` | Payment plan details |
| `lossSummary` | `LossSummary?` | Loss history summary |
| `individualClaims` | `ClaimRecord[]?` | Individual claim records |
| `experienceMod` | `ExperienceMod?` | Experience modification |

### PolicyDocument fields

In addition to the shared fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `type` | `"policy"` | Always `"policy"` |
| `policyNumber` | `string` | Policy number |
| `effectiveDate` | `string` | Policy effective date |
| `expirationDate` | `string?` | Policy expiration date |
| `effectiveTime` | `string?` | Time coverage begins |
| `policyTermType` | `PolicyTermType?` | Annual, semi-annual, etc. |
| `nextReviewDate` | `string?` | Next review date |
| `isRenewal` | `boolean?` | Whether this is a renewal |
| `isPackage` | `boolean?` | Whether this is a commercial package |
| `priorPolicyNumber` | `string?` | Previous policy number |
| `programName` | `string?` | Named program |

### QuoteDocument fields

In addition to the shared fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `type` | `"quote"` | Always `"quote"` |
| `quoteNumber` | `string` | Quote number |
| `proposedEffectiveDate` | `string?` | Proposed effective date |
| `proposedExpirationDate` | `string?` | Proposed expiration date |
| `quoteExpirationDate` | `string?` | When the quote expires |
| `premiumBreakdown` | `PremiumLine[]?` | Line-by-line premium |
| `subjectivities` | `Subjectivity[]?` | Binding conditions |
| `enrichedSubjectivities` | `EnrichedSubjectivity[]?` | Subjectivities with category, due date, status |
| `underwritingConditions` | `UnderwritingCondition[]?` | Carrier requirements |
| `enrichedUnderwritingConditions` | `EnrichedUnderwritingCondition[]?` | Conditions with category |
| `warrantyRequirements` | `string[]?` | Warranty provisions |
| `lossControlRecommendations` | `string[]?` | Loss control recommendations |
| `bindingAuthority` | `BindingAuthority?` | Binding authority details |

### Type narrowing

Use the `type` discriminator to narrow the union:

```typescript
const { document } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64);

if (document.type === "policy") {
  console.log(document.policyNumber);     // string
  console.log(document.effectiveDate);    // string
} else {
  console.log(document.quoteNumber);      // string
  console.log(document.subjectivities);   // Subjectivity[] | undefined
}
```

## DocumentChunk

The pipeline's `chunks` output contains retrieval-ready chunks produced by `chunkDocument()`. Each chunk is designed for vector storage and semantic search.

```typescript
interface DocumentChunk {
  /** Deterministic ID: `${documentId}:${type}:${index}` */
  id: string;
  /** Source document ID */
  documentId: string;
  /** Chunk type for filtering */
  type: "carrier_info" | "named_insured" | "coverage"
      | "definition" | "covered_reason" | "endorsement"
      | "exclusion" | "condition" | "section" | "declaration"
      | "loss_history" | "premium" | "supplementary"
      | "location" | "vehicle" | "classification" | "financial"
      | "party" | "subjectivity" | "underwriting_condition";
  /** Human-readable text for embedding */
  text: string;
  /** Structured metadata for filtering */
  metadata: Record<string, string>;
}
```

### Chunk types

| Type | Source | Content |
|------|--------|---------|
| `carrier_info` | Carrier extractor | Carrier name, NAIC, AM Best, MGA, underwriter, broker |
| `named_insured` | Named insured extractor | Insured name, DBA, address, entity type |
| `coverage` | Coverage limits extractor | One chunk per coverage with limits and deductibles |
| `definition` | Definitions extractor | One chunk per defined term |
| `covered_reason` | Covered reasons extractor | One chunk per covered cause/reason plus one chunk per attached condition |
| `endorsement` | Endorsements extractor | One chunk per endorsement with form number and content |
| `exclusion` | Exclusions extractor | One chunk per exclusion |
| `condition` | Conditions extractor | One chunk per policy condition |
| `section` | Sections extractor | Raw section content |
| `declaration` | Declarations extractor | Line-specific declaration data |
| `loss_history` | Loss history extractor | Loss runs and claim records |
| `premium` | Premium breakdown extractor | Premium, taxes, fees, payment plans |
| `supplementary` | Supplementary extractor | Regulatory context, TPA, contacts, and hidden retrieval-only facts |

### Hidden supplementary facts

The extraction pipeline runs a second-pass `supplementary` extraction across the full document after the page-mapped strict extractors finish. The goal is to preserve useful facts that do not belong in the primary visible schema but are still valuable for memory and retrieval.

Those facts are stored on the document as `supplementaryFacts` and are also embedded into the `supplementary` chunk type for vector search.

Typical examples include:

- `policyholder_name`
- `insured_name`
- `driver_age`
- `driver_date_of_birth`
- `garaging_zip`
- `vehicle_principal_driver`

Each fact is a structured record:

```typescript
{
  key: string;
  value: string;
  subject?: string;
  context?: string;
}
```

Use `subject` to associate the fact with a specific person, vehicle, or scheduled item, and `context` to keep light provenance such as `"Driver Schedule"` or `"Named Insured"`.

<Callout type="info">
`supplementaryFacts` are intended for hidden memory and retrieval workflows. The SDK stores them on the returned document for persistence, but consumer applications typically should not render them by default.
</Callout>

### Deterministic chunk IDs

Chunk IDs follow the pattern `${documentId}:${type}:${index}`, where `index` is a zero-based counter within each type. This makes chunk IDs deterministic and idempotent — re-extracting the same document produces the same chunk IDs, enabling safe upserts.

```typescript
// Example chunk IDs for document "doc-123":
// "doc-123:carrier_info:0"
// "doc-123:coverage:0"
// "doc-123:coverage:1"
// "doc-123:coverage:2"
// "doc-123:endorsement:0"
// "doc-123:endorsement:1"
```

## Persisting results

The `ExtractionResult` is designed to feed directly into the SDK's storage interfaces:

```typescript

const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
});

const { document, chunks, tokenUsage, usageReporting } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64, "doc-123");

// Save structured document to DocumentStore
await documentStore.save(document);

// Save chunks to MemoryStore for vector search
await memoryStore.addChunks(chunks);
```

<Callout type="info">
`DocumentStore` and `MemoryStore` are interfaces defined by the SDK. The SDK includes a reference SQLite implementation, but you can implement these interfaces with any storage backend.
</Callout>

## Token usage

The `tokenUsage` field on `ExtractionResult` provides cumulative input and output token counts across all model calls that actually return usage metadata.

```typescript
interface TokenUsage {
  inputTokens: number;
  outputTokens: number;
}
```

```typescript
const { tokenUsage, usageReporting } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64);
console.log(`Cost: ${tokenUsage.inputTokens} input + ${tokenUsage.outputTokens} output tokens`);
console.log(`Usage reported for ${usageReporting.callsWithUsage}/${usageReporting.modelCalls} model calls`);
```

For per-call tracking, use the `onTokenUsage` callback in `ExtractorConfig`. If some provider calls omit usage, `usageReporting.callsMissingUsage` will make that visible.

## Declarations promotion

The pipeline's Promote phase bridges the gap between raw extraction and typed output. Extractors capture detailed data — often 100+ declaration fields, dozens of coverages with individual limits and deductibles — but the initial assembly only spreads flat results. The promotion pass scans this data and fills typed top-level fields:

- **Carrier fields** — Maps extractor short names (`naicNumber`, `admittedStatus`, `amBestRating`) to canonical schema names (`carrierNaicNumber`, `carrierAdmittedStatus`, `carrierAmBestRating`)
- **Broker/producer** — Promotes broker data from declarations or carrier extractor into `brokerAgency`, `brokerContactName`, `producer`
- **Loss payees and mortgage holders** — Promotes from declarations into `lossPayees[]` and `mortgageHolders[]`
- **Locations** — Groups location-related declaration fields by location number into `locations[]`
- **Limits schedule** — Synthesizes `limits` from well-known coverage name patterns (GL, auto, umbrella, workers comp)
- **Deductible schedule** — Finds the base deductible and SIR from coverage data into `deductibles`
- **Financial fallback** — Promotes premium and total cost from declaration aliases if the premium extractor missed them, and synthesizes `taxesAndFees[]` from declaration tax/fee/surcharge/assessment fields

All sub-passes are idempotent — they never overwrite values that were directly extracted.

## Null handling

The assembler uses `sanitizeNulls` internally to convert `null` values to `undefined` throughout the document. This ensures compatibility with frameworks like Convex that reject `null` for optional fields. You do not need to call `sanitizeNulls` yourself on extraction results.

pages.title: Classification
pages.description: How the extraction pipeline classifies insurance documents by type and line of business
pages.content:
Classification is the first phase of the extraction pipeline. It automatically determines whether a document is a policy or quote and identifies which lines of business are covered. Classification drives template selection in the Plan phase, which determines which extractors run and what fields are expected.

<Callout type="info">
Classification runs automatically when you call `extractor.extract()`. There is no separate classification function to call — it is integrated into the pipeline.
</Callout>

## What the classifier detects

The classifier uses `generateObject` with the `ClassifyResultSchema` to return three fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `documentType` | `"policy" \| "quote"` | Whether the document represents bound coverage or proposed coverage |
| `policyTypes` | `string[]` | Lines of business found (e.g., `["general_liability", "commercial_auto"]`) |
| `confidence` | `number` | Confidence score from 0 to 1 |

For this step, the coordinator passes the full PDF through `providerOptions.pdfBase64`. Your `generateObject` callback must attach that PDF to the provider request so the model can actually inspect the document.

### ClassifyResult schema

```typescript

const ClassifyResultSchema = z.object({
  documentType: z.enum(["policy", "quote"]),
  policyTypes: z.array(PolicyTypeSchema),
  confidence: z.number(),
});
```

## How classification drives the pipeline

The classification result determines everything downstream:

1. **Template selection** — The primary policy type (first element of `policyTypes`) selects a line-of-business template that defines expected sections, page hints, and required fields
2. **Extraction plan** — The Plan phase uses template hints to assign focused extractors to specific page ranges
3. **Review criteria** — The Review phase checks completeness against the template's required fields
4. **Document assembly** — The Assemble phase creates either a `PolicyDocument` or `QuoteDocument` based on `documentType`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Classify] -->|documentType + policyTypes| B[Select Template]
    B -->|expectedSections, pageHints| C[Plan Tasks]
    C -->|extractor assignments| D[Extract]
    D --> E[Review vs. required]
    E --> F[Assemble PolicyDocument or QuoteDocument]
```

## Classification signals

The classifier examines the first few pages of the document and looks for signals such as:

**Policy signals:**
- Policy numbers and declarations pages
- Effective/expiration dates
- Premium charges and bound coverage language

**Quote signals:**
- Quote or proposal numbers
- "Indication" or "proposal" language
- Subjectivities and binding conditions
- Proposed dates

## Supported policy types

The SDK supports 42 policy types across commercial, personal, and specialty lines:

### Commercial lines

| Policy type | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `general_liability` | Commercial general liability |
| `commercial_property` | Commercial property |
| `commercial_auto` | Commercial auto |
| `non_owned_auto` | Non-owned / hired auto |
| `workers_comp` | Workers' compensation |
| `umbrella` | Commercial umbrella |
| `excess_liability` | Excess liability |
| `professional_liability` | Professional liability / E&O |
| `cyber` | Cyber liability |
| `epli` | Employment practices liability |
| `directors_officers` | Directors & officers |
| `fiduciary_liability` | Fiduciary liability |
| `crime_fidelity` | Crime / fidelity |
| `inland_marine` | Inland marine |
| `builders_risk` | Builders risk |
| `environmental` | Environmental liability |
| `ocean_marine` | Ocean marine |
| `surety` | Surety bonds |
| `product_liability` | Product liability |
| `bop` | Business owners policy |
| `management_liability_package` | Management liability package |
| `property` | Property (standalone) |

### Personal lines

| Policy type | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `homeowners_ho3` | Homeowners HO-3 |
| `homeowners_ho5` | Homeowners HO-5 |
| `renters_ho4` | Renters HO-4 |
| `condo_ho6` | Condo HO-6 |
| `dwelling_fire` | Dwelling fire |
| `mobile_home` | Mobile home |
| `personal_auto` | Personal auto |
| `personal_umbrella` | Personal umbrella |
| `flood_nfip` | Flood (NFIP) |
| `flood_private` | Flood (private) |
| `earthquake` | Earthquake |
| `personal_inland_marine` | Personal inland marine |
| `watercraft` | Watercraft |
| `recreational_vehicle` | Recreational vehicle |
| `farm_ranch` | Farm & ranch |

### Specialty

| Policy type | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `pet` | Pet insurance |
| `travel` | Travel insurance |
| `identity_theft` | Identity theft |
| `title` | Title insurance |
| `other` | Unrecognized policy type |

## Multi-line documents

Many commercial documents cover multiple lines of business (e.g., a commercial package policy with GL, property, and auto). The classifier returns all detected policy types in the `policyTypes` array.

The pipeline uses the **first** policy type as the primary type for template selection. The template's expected sections and page hints are tuned to that primary line, but all extractors still run against the full document, capturing data from all lines present.

pages.title: Form Inventory
pages.description: How the form inventory phase constrains page mapping and extraction planning
pages.content:
# Form Inventory and Extraction Planning

**Version:** 1.0
**Last Updated:** 2026-04-12
**Scope:** How the form inventory phase constrains page mapping and extraction planning

---

## Purpose

Insurance documents contain dozens of distinct forms — declarations pages, coverage forms, endorsements, notices, and applications — often interleaved across hundreds of pages. Without structural awareness, the page mapper can misassign pages, sending endorsement form language to the `coverage_limits` extractor (producing phantom coverages from referential wording) or missing endorsement pages entirely.

The **form inventory** phase catalogs every form in the document before page mapping begins, giving both the LLM page mapper and the deterministic normalizer a structural map to work with.

---

## Pipeline Phases

The extraction pipeline runs in this order:

```
1. Classify        → document type + policy types
2. Form Inventory  → catalog all forms with page ranges     ← NEW
3. Page Map        → assign extractors per page (inventory-aware)
4. Plan            → build extraction tasks from page map
5. Extract         → dispatch extractors in parallel
6. Review          → LLM review loop + deterministic quality checks
7. Assemble        → build InsuranceDocument
8. Format          → markdown content generation
```

### Phase 2: Form Inventory

**File:** `src/prompts/coordinator/form-inventory.ts`

The form inventory prompt asks the model to scan the full document and produce a list of every distinct form it can identify:

```ts
interface FormInventoryEntry {
  formNumber: string;       // e.g. "PR5070CF", "CG 00 01"
  editionDate?: string;     // e.g. "01 24"
  title?: string;           // e.g. "Commercial Property Coverage Form"
  formType: "coverage" | "endorsement" | "declarations" | "application" | "notice" | "other";
  pageStart?: number;       // original document page where form begins
  pageEnd?: number;         // original document page where form ends
}
```

This schema extends `FormReferenceSchema` from `src/schemas/shared.ts`, so the output is directly assignable to the document's `formInventory` field without transformation.

The inventory is checkpointed as `form_inventory` and stored in extraction memory for downstream use by the review report.

### Phase 3: Page Map (Inventory-Aware)

**File:** `src/prompts/coordinator/page-map.ts`

The page mapper receives the form inventory as a prompt hint when available:

```
FORM INVENTORY (already identified — use this to constrain your assignments):
- PR5070CF "Commercial Property Coverage Form" [coverage] → pages 7-36
- PR068END "Leasehold Interest" [endorsement] → pages 37-38
```

This gives the model structural context before it assigns extractors per page. The prompt also includes a rule:

> When a form inventory entry identifies a page range as a specific form type, use that classification to guide your extractor choice. Do not assign "coverage_limits" to pages the inventory identifies as endorsement or condition/exclusion forms unless the page contains actual schedule values.

**Formatting helper:** `formatFormInventoryForPageMap()` converts `FormInventoryEntry[]` into the concise prompt block shown above. Only entries with `pageStart` are included.

### Deterministic Normalization

After the LLM produces page assignments, `normalizePageAssignments()` applies hard constraints using the form inventory:

1. **Block `coverage_limits`** on pages the inventory identifies as endorsement, notice, or application forms — unless `hasScheduleValues` is true or the page also has `declarations` assigned.
2. **Ensure `endorsements`** is assigned to pages the inventory identifies as endorsement forms, even if the LLM missed it.
3. **Existing role-based rules** still apply: `coverage_limits` is dropped from pages with `pageRole` of `policy_form`, `condition_exclusion_form`, or `endorsement_form`.

This two-layer approach (soft prompt constraint + hard deterministic override) prevents the most common misassignment patterns.

---

## Coverage Provenance

Coverage rows extracted by the `coverage_limits` extractor now carry provenance fields:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `pageNumber` | `number` | Original document page where the coverage row appears |
| `sectionRef` | `string` | Declarations/schedule/endorsement heading |
| `originalContent` | `string` | Verbatim source text used for extraction |
| `limitValueType` | `CoverageValueType` | Classifies the limit: `numeric`, `included`, `not_included`, `as_stated`, `waiting_period`, `referential`, `other` |
| `deductibleValueType` | `CoverageValueType` | Same classification for the deductible term |

These fields are defined in `CoverageSchema` (`src/schemas/coverage.ts`) and flow through to:
- `EnrichedCoverageSchema` — carries `limitValueType`, `deductibleValueType`, `originalContent`
- Document chunks — included in chunk text and `metadata`
- Quality reports — missing provenance triggers `coverage_missing_page_number`, `coverage_missing_section_ref`, `coverage_missing_original_content` warnings
- Merge — `mergeShallowPreferPresent` preserves provenance when duplicate coverage rows are merged

### Coverage Value Types

The `CoverageValueType` enum preserves non-numeric limit/deductible terms instead of forcing them into dollar-value semantics:

| Value | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `numeric` | Standard dollar amount (e.g. `$1,000,000`) |
| `included` | Explicitly included in coverage |
| `not_included` | Explicitly excluded from coverage |
| `as_stated` | References another document/section |
| `waiting_period` | Time-based deductible (e.g. `72 Hours`) |
| `referential` | Points to declarations/schedule for the actual value |
| `other` | None of the above |

---

## Prompt Improvements

### Coverage Limits Extractor

The `coverage_limits` prompt (`src/prompts/extractors/coverage-limits.ts`) now:
- Requires provenance fields (`pageNumber`, `sectionRef`, `originalContent`) for every coverage
- Instructs the model to classify limit/deductible terms using `limitValueType`/`deductibleValueType`
- Explicitly blocks extraction from TOC lines, generic form text, and referential wording
- Uses `CoverageSchema` from the canonical schema as its base, ensuring type alignment

### Conditions, Exclusions, Sections Extractors

All three extractors now include "Critical rules" blocks that:
- Ignore table-of-contents entries, running headers/footers, and page references
- Require substantive content (not just a heading or TOC line) before emitting an item
- Handle continuation fragments by keeping text together rather than creating orphan entries

---

## Schema Alignment

The form inventory, coverage, and quality report schemas are aligned to prevent drift:

| Schema | Source | Used By |
|--------|--------|---------|
| `FormReferenceSchema` | `src/schemas/shared.ts` | Document's `formInventory` field, form inventory prompt |
| `FormInventoryEntrySchema` | Extends `FormReferenceSchema` | Form inventory extraction prompt output |
| `CoverageSchema` | `src/schemas/coverage.ts` | Document's `coverages`, coverage limits extractor output |
| `CoverageValueTypeSchema` | `src/schemas/coverage.ts` | `CoverageSchema`, `EnrichedCoverageSchema`, extractor prompt |
| `ApplicationQualityReportSchema` | `src/schemas/application.ts` | `ApplicationState` serialization |

pages.title: Extraction Pipeline
pages.description: Turn insurance PDFs into structured data with the agentic extraction pipeline
pages.content:
CL SDK's extraction pipeline uses an agentic coordinator/worker pattern to process insurance documents. It classifies the document, maps pages to focused extractors, builds deterministic tasks, dispatches focused extractors in parallel, runs a full-document supplementary pass for hidden retrieval facts, merges repeated extractor runs, reviews for completeness and quality, assembles a fully typed `InsuranceDocument`, promotes declarations data into typed top-level fields, generates a document summary, and produces retrieval-ready chunks.

## Quick start

```typescript

const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,   // Your provider callback
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
  onProgress: (msg) => console.log(msg),
});

const { document, chunks, tokenUsage, usageReporting } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64, "doc-123");

console.log(document.type);       // "policy" | "quote"
console.log(document.carrier);    // "Hartford"
console.log(chunks.length);       // 42
console.log(tokenUsage);          // { inputTokens: 85000, outputTokens: 12000 }
console.log(usageReporting);      // { modelCalls: 14, callsWithUsage: 14, callsMissingUsage: 0 }
```

<Callout type="info">
CL SDK is provider-agnostic. You supply plain callback functions (`GenerateText`, `GenerateObject`) that wrap your preferred provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.). The SDK never imports any provider package.
</Callout>

## Pipeline overview

The pipeline runs ten phases sequentially. Extraction and review dispatch work in parallel internally.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PDF Input] --> B[1. Classify]
    B --> C[2. Page Map]
    C --> D[3. Plan]
    D --> E[4. Extract + Merge]
    E --> F[5. Review]
    F -->|gaps found| E
    F -->|complete| G[6. Assemble]
    G --> H[7. Promote]
    H --> I[8. Summarize]
    I --> J[9. Format]
    J --> K[10. Chunk]
    K --> L[InsuranceDocument + DocumentChunks]
```

## Phase 1: Classify

The classifier examines the document and determines:

- **Document type** — `"policy"` (bound coverage) or `"quote"` (proposed coverage)
- **Policy types** — array of line-of-business types (e.g., `["general_liability", "commercial_auto"]`)
- **Confidence** — numeric score from 0 to 1

Classification uses `generateObject` with the `ClassifyResultSchema` and drives template selection in the next phase. The coordinator passes the full PDF through `providerOptions.pdfBase64` for this step. See [Classification](/docs/extraction/classification) for details.

## Phase 2: Page Map

The coordinator first maps each page to one or more focused extractors. This is the step that prevents broad, mixed ranges from sending declaration pages and generic policy forms through the same extractor without distinction.

The page mapper receives chunked PDFs through `providerOptions.pdfBase64` and assigns pages one by one.

## Phase 3: Plan

The planner selects a line-of-business template based on the primary policy type and builds an extraction plan from the page map — a list of tasks that map focused extractors to page ranges.

Each task specifies:
- **Extractor name** — which focused extractor to run (e.g., `declarations`, `coverage_limits`)
- **Page range** — `startPage` and `endPage` for the extractor to focus on

The planner receives template hints including expected sections, page hints, and total page count. In the current coordinator, the plan is built deterministically from the page map rather than delegated to the older broad prompt-only planning path.

### Line-of-business templates

The SDK includes 20 templates covering 42 policy types. Each template defines:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `expectedSections` | Sections the template expects to find (e.g., `["declarations", "coverages", "conditions"]`) |
| `pageHints` | Guidance on where sections typically appear (e.g., `"declarations: pages 1-3"`) |
| `required` | Fields that must be extracted for completeness |
| `optional` | Fields that may or may not be present |

**Available templates:**

| Template | Policy types |
|----------|-------------|
| General Liability | `general_liability` |
| Commercial Property | `commercial_property` |
| Commercial Auto | `commercial_auto`, `non_owned_auto` |
| Workers' Comp | `workers_comp` |
| Umbrella / Excess | `umbrella`, `excess_liability` |
| Professional Liability | `professional_liability` |
| Cyber | `cyber` |
| D&O | `directors_officers` |
| EPLI | `epli` |
| Crime | `crime_fidelity` |
| Inland Marine | `inland_marine` |
| Builders Risk | `builders_risk` |
| Environmental | `environmental` |
| Homeowners | `homeowners_ho3`, `homeowners_ho5`, `renters_ho4`, `condo_ho6` |
| Personal Auto | `personal_auto` |
| Dwelling Fire | `dwelling_fire`, `mobile_home` |
| Flood | `flood_nfip`, `flood_private` |
| Personal Umbrella | `personal_umbrella` |
| Farm & Ranch | `farm_ranch` |
| Default | All other types |

Templates are accessed internally via `getTemplate(policyType)`.

## Phase 4: Extract And Merge

The coordinator dispatches focused extractors in parallel (concurrency-limited, default 2). Each extractor targets a specific data domain against the page range assigned by the plan.

Results accumulate in an in-memory `Map` keyed by extractor name.

Before each worker callback is invoked, the SDK slices the assigned page range with `extractPageRange()` and passes that page-scoped PDF through `providerOptions.pdfBase64`. If `convertPdfToImages` is configured, it passes `providerOptions.images` instead.

If the same extractor runs multiple times, results merge instead of overwrite. This is especially important for `coverage_limits`, `definitions`, `covered_reasons`, `endorsements`, `exclusions`, `conditions`, `sections`, `declarations`, and `supplementary`.

### The 13 focused extractors

| Extractor | Domain | What it extracts |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| `carrier_info` | Carrier | Carrier name, NAIC, AM Best, MGA, underwriter, broker/producer agency and license |
| `named_insured` | Insured | Insured name, DBA, address, entity type, FEIN, SIC/NAICS, loss payees, mortgage holders |
| `declarations` | Declarations | Line-specific structured declarations (varies by policy type) |
| `coverage_limits` | Coverages | Coverage names, limits, deductibles, forms, triggers |
| `definitions` | Definitions | Defined terms, full definition text, form/page provenance |
| `covered_reasons` | Covered reasons | Covered causes of loss, covered losses, inclusions, qualifying reasons, attached conditions/exceptions |
| `endorsements` | Endorsements | Form numbers, titles, types, content, affected parties |
| `exclusions` | Exclusions | Exclusion titles, content, applicability |
| `conditions` | Conditions | Duties after loss, cancellation, other insurance, etc. |
| `premium_breakdown` | Premium | Premiums, taxes, fees, payment plans, rating basis |
| `loss_history` | Loss history | Loss runs, claim records, experience modification |
| `supplementary` | Supplementary | Regulatory context, contacts, TPA, claims contacts, and hidden retrieval-only facts |
| `sections` | Sections | Raw section content (fallback for unmatched sections) |

Each extractor is defined with `{ buildPrompt(), schema, maxTokens }` and accessed via `getExtractor(name)`.

### Hidden supplementary facts pass

After the page-mapped extractor tasks complete, the coordinator runs a full-document `supplementary` pass across the entire PDF. This pass is intended for memory and retrieval rather than consumer-facing structured output.

The pass still captures regulatory contacts and notice periods, but it also emits `auxiliaryFacts`: normalized key/value facts that do not fit the strict primary schema.

Typical `auxiliaryFacts` include:

- policyholder names
- insured or driver names
- ages and dates of birth
- marital status
- garaging ZIPs
- vehicle-to-driver assignments
- household members
- lienholder and schedule row details

Each auxiliary fact can include optional `subject` and `context` fields so downstream agents can distinguish document-level facts from person-specific or schedule-specific facts.

This data is assembled onto the final document as `supplementaryFacts` and chunked for vector retrieval, but it is meant to remain hidden from normal consumer UI rendering unless your application explicitly chooses to surface it.

## Phase 5: Review

After initial extraction, a review loop (up to `maxReviewRounds`, default 2) checks completeness and quality against the template's `required` fields. The reviewer compares what has been extracted against what is expected, sees the document again, and receives a summary of extracted outputs and page assignments.

If gaps or quality issues are found, the reviewer generates additional extraction tasks. These follow-up extractors are dispatched in parallel, just like the main extraction phase. The loop continues until either:
- All required fields are present, or
- The maximum review rounds are reached

Typical quality issues include generic placeholders like "shown in declarations", "per schedule", values that look copied from generic form wording instead of declaration-specific schedules, mapped definitions pages with no definition records, and mapped covered-cause/inclusion pages with no covered reason records.

## Phase 6: Assemble

The assembler merges all extractor results from the in-memory `Map` into a single validated `InsuranceDocument` (either `PolicyDocument` or `QuoteDocument`).

## Phase 7: Promote

After assembly, a promotion pass fills in top-level typed fields from data that was extracted but not directly surfaced. The extractors do detailed work — 100+ declaration fields, dozens of coverages — but the initial assembly only spreads flat results. The promotion pass bridges this gap.

The promoter runs eight sub-passes, all pure data transformations with no model calls:

| Sub-pass | What it does |
|----------|-------------|
| **Carrier field mapping** | Maps extractor short names to canonical schema names (`naicNumber` → `carrierNaicNumber`, `admittedStatus` → `carrierAdmittedStatus`, `amBestRating` → `carrierAmBestRating`). Also builds the structured `insurer` sub-object. |
| **Broker promotion** | Scans declarations for broker/agent/producer fields and promotes to `brokerAgency`, `brokerContactName`, `brokerLicenseNumber`, and the structured `producer` object. Falls back to declarations if the carrier extractor didn't capture broker data. |
| **Loss payee / mortgage holder promotion** | Scans declarations for loss payees and mortgage holders and populates `lossPayees[]` and `mortgageHolders[]` as `EndorsementParty` objects. |
| **Location promotion** | Groups location-related declaration fields (address, construction type, occupancy, building value, etc.) by location number and populates the `locations[]` array. |
| **Limits synthesis** | Maps well-known coverage names (e.g. "Each Occurrence", "General Aggregate", "Products/Completed Operations Aggregate") from the `coverages[]` array into the structured `limits` schedule. Handles GL, auto, umbrella, workers comp, and employers liability patterns. |
| **Deductible synthesis** | Finds the most common deductible across coverages as the base `perOccurrence` deductible. Detects self-insured retentions and aggregate deductibles. |
| **Taxes and fees synthesis** | Converts declaration tax, fee, surcharge, and assessment fields into structured `taxesAndFees[]` entries, including common GST/HST/PST/QST-style labels. |
| **Premium and total-cost fallback** | If the premium extractor didn't find financial data, promotes premium and total-cost values from declaration aliases such as `premiumAmount`, `totalPaid`, `amountPaid`, and `amountCharged`. Tax fields are excluded from premium promotion. |

All sub-passes are idempotent — they never overwrite fields that already have values from direct extraction.

<Callout type="info">
  The promotion pass runs in `src/extraction/promote.ts` and is called automatically by `assembleDocument()`. It produces no model calls and adds no latency.
</Callout>

## Phase 8: Summarize

After promotion, the pipeline generates a 1–3 sentence summary of the document using a model call. The summary captures the essential facts a broker or underwriter would want at a glance: who is insured, by whom, key coverages, policy period, and premium.

The summarizer receives a compact snapshot of the assembled document (not the full PDF), so it adds minimal token cost. The `summary` field appears on the final `InsuranceDocument`.

If summary generation fails, the pipeline continues without it — the field remains `undefined`.

## Phase 9: Format

After assembly, a formatting agent pass cleans up markdown in all content-bearing string fields — section content, subsection content, definition text, covered-reason content and conditions, endorsement content, exclusion content, condition content, and the document summary.

Insurance document extraction commonly produces content with formatting issues that prevent proper markdown rendering:

| Issue | Example | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| Pipe tables missing separator rows | `COVERAGE \| LIMIT \| DEDUCTIBLE` with no `\| --- \| --- \|` | Add separator row, leading/trailing pipes |
| Space-aligned tables | Columns padded with whitespace instead of pipes | Convert to proper markdown table syntax |
| Sub-items mixed into tables | Indented lines like `  Waiting Period (Hours): 24` inside a pipe table | Pull sub-items out into lists |
| Mixed table/prose content | Paragraphs followed by tables followed by more paragraphs | Handle each segment independently |
| General cleanup | Excessive blank lines, trailing whitespace, orphaned `**` or `*` markers | Standard markdown normalization |

Content fields are collected and batched (up to 20 per model call) for efficient processing. The formatting prompt includes specific before/after examples for each pattern. Token usage from formatting is tracked and included in the total.

<Callout type="info">
  The formatter only fixes formatting — it never changes the meaning or substance of extracted content. Dollar amounts, dates, policy numbers, and technical terms are preserved exactly as extracted.
</Callout>

## Phase 10: Chunk

The formatted document is broken into retrieval-ready `DocumentChunk` objects via `chunkDocument()`. Chunks inherit the cleaned formatting, ensuring consistent rendering in downstream applications like query results and chat UIs.

Definitions and covered reasons produce dedicated `definition` and `covered_reason` chunks so retrieval can answer questions like "what does this term mean?" or "what losses are covered?" without depending on generic section text. Covered-reason conditions are chunked separately under the same `covered_reason` type.

If supplementary data exists, `chunkDocument()` also emits a `supplementary` chunk containing claims and regulatory contacts plus any hidden `supplementaryFacts`, making those facts searchable by agents without polluting the primary visible fields.

The final `ExtractionResult` contains:

```typescript
interface ExtractionResult {
  document: InsuranceDocument;  // Fully typed policy or quote
  chunks: DocumentChunk[];       // Ready for vector storage
  tokenUsage: TokenUsage;        // Cumulative { inputTokens, outputTokens }
  usageReporting: {
    modelCalls: number;
    callsWithUsage: number;
    callsMissingUsage: number;
  };
}
```

See [Extraction Results](/docs/extraction/applying-results) for details on working with the output.

## Configuration

### ExtractorConfig

```typescript
interface ExtractorConfig {
  generateText: GenerateText;                    // Required — text generation callback
  generateObject: GenerateObject;                // Required — structured output callback
  convertPdfToImages?: ConvertPdfToImagesFn;     // Optional — send pages as images instead of PDF
  concurrency?: number;                          // Default: 2
  maxReviewRounds?: number;                      // Default: 2
  onTokenUsage?: (usage: TokenUsage) => void;    // Per-call token usage callback
  onProgress?: (message: string) => void;        // Progress message callback
  log?: LogFn;                                   // Debug logging callback
  providerOptions?: Record<string, unknown>;     // Pass-through to provider
}
```

### Provider callbacks

```typescript
type GenerateText = (params: {
  prompt: string;
  system?: string;
  maxTokens: number;
  providerOptions?: Record<string, unknown>;
}) => Promise<{ text: string; usage?: TokenUsage }>;

type GenerateObject<T = unknown> = (params: {
  prompt: string;
  system?: string;
  schema: ZodSchema<T>;
  maxTokens: number;
  providerOptions?: Record<string, unknown>;
}) => Promise<{ object: T; usage?: TokenUsage }>;
```

Your callback must treat `providerOptions` as part of the model input contract, not just optional metadata. In the extraction pipeline, the document reaches the model through `providerOptions.pdfBase64` or `providerOptions.images`.

### Tuning concurrency

The default concurrency of 2 balances throughput against rate limits. Increase it for higher-tier API plans:

```typescript
const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
  concurrency: 4,  // Up to 4 extractors in parallel
});
```

## Token usage tracking

Track cumulative token usage across the entire pipeline:

```typescript
let totalInput = 0, totalOutput = 0;

const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
  onTokenUsage: ({ inputTokens, outputTokens }) => {
    totalInput += inputTokens;
    totalOutput += outputTokens;
  },
});

const { tokenUsage, usageReporting } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64);
console.log(`Total: ${tokenUsage.inputTokens} input, ${tokenUsage.outputTokens} output`);
console.log(`Usage reported for ${usageReporting.callsWithUsage}/${usageReporting.modelCalls} model calls`);
```

The `onTokenUsage` callback fires after every model call that returns usage metadata, making it useful for cost monitoring. The `tokenUsage` field on the result provides the cumulative total, and `usageReporting` tells you whether some provider calls omitted usage entirely.

## Progress callbacks

Monitor pipeline progress in real time:

```typescript
const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
  onProgress: (message) => {
    // "Classifying document..."
    // "Mapping document pages for commercial_auto policy..."
    // "Building extraction plan from page map for commercial_auto policy..."
    // "Dispatching 8 extractors..."
    // "Extracting carrier_info (pages 1-3)..."
    // "Extracting coverage_limits (pages 4-12)..."
    // "Review round 1: dispatching 2 follow-up extractors..."
    // "Extraction complete."
    // "Assembling document..."
    // "Formatting extracted content..."
    // "Formatting 24 content fields..."
    console.log(message);
  },
});
```

## PDF input modes

By default, the SDK sends PDF content directly to the model. If your provider works better with images, supply a `convertPdfToImages` callback:

```typescript
const extractor = createExtractor({
  generateText: myGenerateText,
  generateObject: myGenerateObject,
  convertPdfToImages: async (pdfBase64, startPage, endPage) => {
    // Convert pages to images using your preferred library
    return pages.map((page) => ({
      imageBase64: page.base64,
      mimeType: "image/png",
    }));
  },
});
```

## Schema compatibility

The SDK automatically handles compatibility with strict structured output APIs like OpenAI. Before every `generateObject` call, schemas are transformed by `toStrictSchema()` which converts `.optional()` fields to `.nullable()`. This ensures all properties appear in the JSON Schema `required` array — a requirement for OpenAI's strict mode.

This happens transparently inside the pipeline. You don't need to modify your schemas or provider adapter.

<Callout type="warn">
  **Never use `z.record()` in schemas passed to `generateObject`** — it generates `propertyNames` in JSON Schema, which is unsupported by most structured output APIs. Use `z.array(z.object({ key: z.string(), value: z.string() }))` instead.
</Callout>

For custom pipelines, you can use `safeGenerateObject()` which wraps `generateObject` with schema strictification, retry on transient errors, and optional fallback values:

```typescript

const { object } = await safeGenerateObject(
  myGenerateObject,
  { prompt: "...", schema: MySchema, maxTokens: 1024 },
  { fallback: { field: "default" }, maxRetries: 2 },
);
```

## Retry resilience

All model calls are wrapped with `withRetry()`, which provides exponential backoff on transient errors. Retry behavior:

- Up to 5 retries
- Delays: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 32s (plus random jitter)
- Non-retryable errors are re-thrown immediately
- Retry attempts are logged via the `log` callback

Retryable errors include:
- Rate limits (HTTP 429, "rate limit" / "too many requests")
- Grammar compilation timeouts
- No output generated
- Server overloaded
- HTTP 500, 502, 503, 504

## Utility functions

### `stripFences(text)`

Removes markdown code fences from AI responses before JSON parsing:

```typescript

stripFences('```json\n{"key": "value"}\n```');
// '{"key": "value"}'
```

### `sanitizeNulls(obj)`

Recursively converts `null` values to `undefined`. Required for frameworks like Convex that reject `null` for optional fields:

```typescript

sanitizeNulls({ a: null, b: [null, 1] });
// { a: undefined, b: [undefined, 1] }
```

pages.title: Architecture
pages.description: How CL SDK's pipelines and composable systems work
pages.content:
CL SDK is organized into five systems: **document extraction**, **query agent**, **application processing**, **agent prompts**, and **storage & memory**. Each is independent — import only what you need.

## Document extraction pipeline

The core of CL SDK is an agentic pipeline that turns insurance PDFs into structured, queryable data. A coordinator classifies the document, maps each page to focused extractors, builds deterministic tasks, merges repeated extractor runs, reviews for completeness and quality, and assembles the final document.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  PDF[PDF Document] --> CL[Classify]
  CL --> PM[Page Map]
  PM --> PL[Plan]
  PL --> EX[Extract + Merge]
  EX --> RV[Review]
  RV --> AS[Assemble]
  RV -.->|gaps found| EX
  AS --> FMT[Format]
  FMT --> CHK[Chunk]
  CHK --> OUT[Document + Chunks]
```

### Classify

Determines document type (policy or quote) and identifies the lines of business present. Uses `generateObject` with structured output for reliable classification, and passes the full document through `providerOptions.pdfBase64`.

### Page map

Maps each page to one or more focused extractors before task construction. This avoids broad mixed ranges where declaration pages and schedule pages get drowned out by generic policy-form language.

### Plan

Builds deterministic extraction tasks from the page map and the selected line-of-business template. The old broad prompt-only planning path is no longer the active coordinator behavior.

### Extract

Dispatches focused extractors in parallel (concurrency-limited, default 2). Each extractor targets a specific data domain against a page range:

- **Declarations** — named insured, policy number, dates, carrier
- **Coverage limits** — coverage names, limits, deductibles, sub-limits
- **Definitions** — defined terms and full definition text with provenance
- **Covered reasons** — covered causes, covered losses, inclusions, and qualifying coverage triggers
- **Conditions** — policy conditions and provisions
- **Endorsements** — endorsement schedules and modifications
- **Exclusions** — exclusion language and applicability
- **Loss history** — prior claims and loss runs
- **Named insured** — insured parties and additional insureds
- **Premium breakdown** — line-by-line premium details
- **Sections** — general document sections with page provenance
- **Supplementary** — regulatory context, contacts, fees
- **Carrier info** — carrier details and AM Best ratings

Results accumulate in an in-memory map as each extractor completes. Repeated extractor runs merge instead of overwrite.

Each worker call is page-scoped. Before invoking the callback, the SDK slices the requested range with `extractPageRange()` and passes that PDF through `providerOptions.pdfBase64`, or passes `providerOptions.images` when `convertPdfToImages` is configured.

### Review

A review loop (up to `maxReviewRounds`, default 2) checks completeness and quality against the template and the extracted results. The reviewer sees the document again and is expected to catch generic placeholders, declaration-free schedule outputs, and other weak extraction artifacts before dispatching focused follow-up tasks.

### Assemble

Merges all extractor results into a final `InsuranceDocument`.

### Format

A post-extraction agent pass cleans up markdown formatting in all content-bearing fields — fixing pipe tables missing separator rows, space-aligned tables, sub-items mixed into tables, and general whitespace issues. This ensures sections, definitions, covered reasons, endorsements, exclusions, and conditions render correctly in downstream UIs.

### Chunk

Breaks the formatted document into `DocumentChunk` objects for vector storage and retrieval.

## Query agent pipeline

The query agent answers user questions against stored documents with citation-backed provenance. It follows the same coordinator/worker pattern as extraction:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Q[User Question] --> C[Classify]
  C --> R[Retrieve]
  R --> RE[Reason]
  RE --> V[Verify]
  V --> RS[Respond]
  V -.->|retry| R
```

1. **Classify** — determine intent and decompose into atomic sub-questions
2. **Retrieve** (parallel) — semantic chunk search, structured document lookup, and conversation history
3. **Reason** (parallel) — answer each sub-question from evidence only, with intent-specific prompts
4. **Verify** — check grounding (every claim has a citation), consistency, and completeness
5. **Respond** — merge sub-answers into a final response with inline citations

The pipeline uses the same provider-agnostic callbacks (`generateText`, `generateObject`), concurrency control, and retry logic as extraction.

## Application processing pipeline

An agentic pipeline for insurance application intake. Eight small, focused agents handle each step, running in parallel where possible:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  PDF[Application PDF] --> C[Classify]
  C --> E[Extract Fields]
  E --> AF[Backfill + Auto-Fill]
  AF --> B[Batch Questions]
  B --> RL[Reply Loop]
  RL --> CF[Confirm + Map PDF]
  RL -.->|next batch| RL
```

1. **Classify** — detect if PDF is an application form (tiny agent, fast model)
2. **Extract fields** — read every field as structured data
3. **Backfill + auto-fill** (parallel) — vector search prior answers, match business context, search document chunks
4. **Batch questions** — organize unfilled fields into topic-based batches
5. **Reply loop** — route reply intent, parse answers / handle lookups / explain fields, advance batch
6. **Confirm + map PDF** — generate confirmation summary, write answers to PDF

Persistent `ApplicationStore` tracks state across the multi-turn collection. `BackfillProvider` enables vector-based answer reuse from prior applications.

## Agent prompt system

A composable system for building insurance-aware conversational agents:

```
buildAgentSystemPrompt(ctx)
  ├── Identity          — agent name, company context
  ├── Intent            — direct / mediated / observed behavior
  ├── Formatting        — platform-specific output rules
  ├── Safety            — scope guardrails, anti-hallucination
  ├── Coverage gaps     — gap detection guidance
  ├── COI routing       — certificate of insurance handling
  ├── Quotes/policies   — document type differentiation
  └── Memory            — cross-conversation continuity
```

Each module is independently importable for custom composition. The system supports five platforms (email, chat, SMS, Slack, Discord) and three communication intents (direct, mediated, observed).

## Design principles

- **Provider-agnostic** — uses plain callback functions (`GenerateText`, `GenerateObject`). Wrap any LLM provider — no framework dependency.
- **Pure TypeScript** — no framework dependencies. Works in Node.js, Deno, edge runtimes.
- **Fail gracefully** — page-aware planning, merged follow-up extraction, review loops that check quality as well as completeness, and adaptive retry with exponential backoff.
- **Schema-only tools** — tool definitions provide schemas without implementations, so consumers control execution.

pages.title: CL SDK
pages.description: Open infrastructure for AI agents working with insurance
pages.content:
[Clarity Labs](https://claritylabs.inc) is an applied AI research lab building the infrastructure for AI to work safely with insurance.

CL SDK is the open infrastructure layer for AI agents working with insurance — a pure TypeScript library for extracting, reasoning about, and acting on insurance documents. Provider-agnostic by design: bring any LLM via plain callback functions. No framework dependency.

## Capabilities

<Cards>
  <Card title="Document Extraction" description="Agentic pipeline with 13 focused extractors that turns insurance PDFs into structured data — coverages, limits, definitions, covered reasons, exclusions, endorsements — with page-level provenance and review loops." />
  <Card title="Query Agent" description="Citation-backed question answering over stored documents plus inbound photos, PDFs, and text. Decomposes questions, retrieves evidence, reasons with intent-specific prompts, and verifies grounding." />
  <Card title="Application Processing" description="Eight focused agents handle application intake — field extraction, auto-fill from prior answers, topic-based question batching, and PDF mapping." />
  <Card title="Agent System" description="Composable prompt modules for building insurance-aware conversational agents across email, chat, SMS, Slack, and Discord." />
  <Card title="Storage & Memory" description="DocumentStore and MemoryStore interfaces for persisting extracted documents, vector chunks, and conversation history. Includes SQLite reference implementation." />
</Cards>

## Install

```bash
npm install @claritylabs/cl-sdk pdf-lib zod
```

```typescript

const extractor = createExtractor({ generateText, generateObject });
const { document, chunks } = await extractor.extract(pdfBase64);

console.log(document.carrier);    // "Hartford"
console.log(document.coverages);  // [{ name: "General Liability", limit: "$1,000,000", ... }]
console.log(chunks.length);       // 24 chunks ready for vector storage
```

## Get started

<Cards>
  <Card title="Quickstart" description="Install CL SDK and run your first extraction in minutes." href="/docs/getting-started/quickstart" />
  <Card title="Architecture" description="Understand the agentic extraction pipeline and how components fit together." href="/docs/getting-started/architecture" />
  <Card title="Provider Callbacks" description="Connect any LLM provider via plain callback functions." href="/docs/getting-started/models" />
  <Card title="Storage" description="Persist documents, chunks, and conversation history." href="/docs/storage" />
  <Card title="API Reference" description="Full reference for every exported function and type." href="/docs/api-reference/extraction" />
</Cards>

pages.title: Overview
pages.description: Search and read CL SDK documentation directly from AI coding assistants via MCP
pages.content:
The CL SDK MCP server provides documentation search and reference as [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) tools. This lets AI coding assistants like Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf look up SDK docs while helping you write integration code.

No API keys or configuration required.

## What it provides

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `search_docs` | Full-text search across all doc pages, returns top 5 matches with context |
| `read_doc_page` | Read a specific doc page by slug (e.g. `getting-started/quickstart`) |
| `list_doc_sections` | List all sections and pages in the doc tree |

## Quick start

Add the server to your MCP client config. No install needed — `npx` fetches it on demand.

<Callout type="info">
  Published on [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp) under the `@claritylabs` scope. Source on [GitHub](https://github.com/claritylabs-inc/cl-sdk-docs/tree/main/mcp).
</Callout>

## Architecture

The server bundles all documentation as `docs-bundle.json` at publish time. At runtime it's a lightweight MCP server with zero external dependencies beyond the MCP SDK. It supports two transport modes:

- **stdio** (default) — for local clients like Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf
- **Streamable HTTP** (`--http` flag) — for remote clients like Claude.ai custom connectors

```
┌──────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  Local clients   │  stdio   │  @claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp        │
│                  │◄────────►│                                 │
│  Claude Code     │          │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │
│  Cursor          │          │  │ search_docs                 │ │
│  Windsurf        │          │  │ read_doc_page               │ │
│                  │          │  │ list_doc_sections           │ │
├──────────────────┤          │  └─────────────┬───────────────┘ │
│  Remote clients  │  HTTP    │                │                 │
│                  │◄────────►│                ▼                 │
│  Claude.ai       │ POST/mcp │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │
│  Custom apps     │          │  │ docs-bundle.json            │ │
└──────────────────┘          │  └─────────────────────────────┘ │
                              └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Client setup

### Claude Code

Add to your project's `.claude/mcp.json` or global `~/.claude/mcp.json`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "cl-sdk": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```

### Cursor / Windsurf

Add to your MCP settings (`~/.cursor/mcp.json` or equivalent):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "cl-sdk": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```

### Claude.ai (remote)

To connect via Claude.ai's **custom connectors**, deploy the server in HTTP mode and point Claude at the URL.

1. Start the server with HTTP transport:

```bash
npx @claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp --http
```

This starts a Streamable HTTP server on port 8787 (configurable via `PORT` env var). The MCP endpoint is `POST /mcp`.

2. In Claude.ai, go to **Settings → Connectors → Add custom connector** and enter your server URL (e.g. `https://your-host.example.com/mcp`).

<Callout type="info">
  The HTTP server is stateless and auth-free — each request gets a fresh server instance. For production, deploy behind a reverse proxy with TLS.
</Callout>

### Self-hosting

Run the HTTP server with Docker or any Node.js hosting:

```bash
# Direct
PORT=8787 npx @claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp --http

# Or via npm start in the mcp directory
npm start
```

| Environment variable | Default | Description |
|---------------------|---------|-------------|
| `PORT` | `8787` | HTTP listen port |
| `HOST` | `0.0.0.0` | Bind address |

### Other MCP clients

Any client that supports stdio servers:

```bash
npx @claritylabs/cl-sdk-mcp
```

</Cards>
